raw_case:
Deep Dermal Dilemma: A Case Report on Majocchi’s Granuloma After Topical Steroid Use
Majocchi’s granuloma represents a unique dermatological entity characterized by the invasion of dermatophytes into the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, typically following trauma or topical corticosteroids, most commonly caused by Trichophyton rubrum. The unrestrained use of over-the-counter topical steroids has led to the rise of Majocchi’s granuloma in the past few years. A 57-year-old male presented with complaints of itchy skin lesions over his right ankle for 20 days. There was a history of over-the-counter topical steroid application, following which the lesions exacerbated. Clinical examination showed multiple ill-defined erythematous nodules, 2-3 cm in size, with serosanguinous discharge associated with right ankle swelling. A single annular hyperpigmented scaly patch was also noted over the right leg below the knee. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) examination was positive for fungal hyphae. Histopathological examination (HPE) of the nodule showed features suggestive of Majocchi's granuloma. Fungal culture was performed, which showed growth of Trichophyton rubrum. The patient was started on oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily and topical luliconazole cream twice daily topical application, which resolved the lesions in three weeks, but the patient was advised to continue the treatment for a total duration of two months.

Introduction

Majocchi’s granuloma is a rare dermatological condition characterized by the invasion of dermatophytes into the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, typically following trauma or topical corticosteroids [1]. It is most commonly caused by Trichophyton rubrum and can involve any hair-bearing area such as the scalp, face, or extremities [2]. Majocchi's granuloma represents a paradox, as dermatophytes usually cause superficial infections limited to stratum corneum. The deeper involvement of dermis makes the disease clinically and pathologically unique among dermatophyte infections [1,2]. The clinical features differ between immunocompetent and immunocompromised individuals [3]. The unrestrained use of over-the-counter topical steroids has led to the rise of Majocchi’s granuloma in the past few years. In this report, we present a rare case of topical steroid-induced Majocchi's granuloma, emphasizing the importance of considering deep dermatophyte infections in patients presenting with atypical nodules over the legs.

Case presentation

A 57-year-old male presented with complaints of itchy erythematous skin lesions over his right ankle for 20 days. There was a history of over-the-counter topical steroid application from the next day onwards till the day of presentation. Though initially, the erythema seemed to be reduced, after five days of topical steroid application, the lesions aggravated with the development of nodules and discharge. Clinical examination showed multiple ill-defined erythematous nodules, 2-3 cm in size, with serosanguinous discharge associated with right ankle swelling. A single annular hyperpigmented scaly patch was also noted over the right leg below the knee (Figure 1).The patient was afebrile and did not have associated regional lymphadenopathy. The systemic examination was also found to be normal. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) examination was positive for fungal hyphae. Histopathological examination (HPE) of the nodule showed mixed inflammatory infiltrate of lymphocytes, macrophages, epithelioid cells, neutrophils, and plasma cells in the deep dermis, with few Langhans giant cells suggestive of Majocchi's granuloma (Figure 2).The discharge was sent for fungal culture sensitivity, which showed growth of Trichophyton rubrum. Based on the clinical, microscopy, and histopathological features, the patient was diagnosed with Majocchi’s granuloma. The patient was started on oral terbinafine 250 mg once daily and topical luliconazole cream twice daily topical application, which resolved the lesions in three weeks, but the patient was advised to continue the treatment for a total duration of two months (Figure 3).

Discussion

Majocchi’s granuloma was first described by Domenico Majocchi in 1883 in Italy, caused by Trichophyton tonsurans [4,5]. Majocchi’s granuloma is also known as Majocchi’s trichophytic granuloma, dermatophytic granuloma, nodular granulomatous perifolliculitis, and granuloma tricofitico [4,5]. It is a deep dermatophytic infection most commonly caused by Trichophyton rubrum. The other causes are described in Table 1 [4,6-10].The predisposing factors include chronic untreated dermatophytosis, impaired skin barrier secondary to trauma, animal exposure, immunocompromised patients, long-term use of steroids, chemotherapeutic drugs, use of adalimumab in organ transplantation patients, and BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib) [6]. A study by Boral et al. showed that topical steroid application for erythematous squamous lesions is the most common predisposing factor for the development of Majocchi’s granuloma accounting for 55% [6]. CARD 9 deficiency is the known risk factor for Majocchi’s granuloma, especially in the Mediterranean region, with the most common organism being Trichophyton violaceum [11].The most important factor triggering the pathogenesis of the disease is physical trauma such as scratching or shaving, leading to fungal invasion. Fungal lysin motif (LysM) domain-associated proteins mask the fungal cell wall chitin, thus protecting it from host immunity. The genes encoding the key components of the glyoxylate pathway and secretion of sulfite destroy components of the skin [6]. The mean age of patients is 38 years, and the mean disease duration of nine months (range: 3 to 60 months) [6]. Females are more affected than males, with a ratio of 3:1 [4]. The disease presents differently in immunocompetent and immunocompromised individuals, as discussed in Table 2 [4,6,12].Majocchi’s granuloma can present as papules, patches, pustules, verrucous plaque, or nodules [7,13]. The most common involved sites are face and lower extremities in immunocompetent and immunocompromised individuals, respectively, with the frequency of facial involvement increasing in the recent past [6]. Palmoplantar hyperkeratosis and erythroderma have been associated with Majocchi’s granuloma in an AIDS patient, caused by Trichophyton tonsurans [14]. Post-inflammatory pigmentation, alopecia, and scarring occur as a sequela of Majocchi’s granuloma [6]. The disease progresses through three clinical phases, based on morphology namely plaques, nodules, and degeneration [4]. The three main criteria to diagnose Majocchi’s granuloma are lesions caused by dermatophytes, perifollicular granulomatous inflammation in histopathological examination, and the presence of dermatophytes in superficial layers and dermis [14,15]. Majocchi's granuloma is usually misdiagnosed initially due to the presence of numerous diseases, with erythematous nodules affecting the extremities especially erythema nodosum, mycetoma, and furunculosis. The differential diagnosis for Majocchi’s granuloma is explained in Table 3 [4,6].The KOH examination shows fungal hyphae and spores, but this cannot differentiate between superficial and deep dermatophytosis [16]. Therefore, histopathological examination is considered the gold standard, which shows perifollicular granulomatous inflammation (granulomatous infiltrate of lymphoid cells, macrophages, epithelioid cells, multinucleated giant cells, neutrophils) [6]. Special stains such as Gomori methenamine silver (GMS) and periodic acid Schiff (PAS) stains aid in the confirmation of diagnosis, with GMS being superior to PAS [6]. Examination of histopathological specimens under an immunofluorescence microscope reveals fungi as auto-fluorescent particles [6]. A negative result in microscopy cannot exclude the diagnosis of Majocchi’s granuloma [12]. Fungal culture can be performed for further confirmation of causative fungi [6]. Molecular methods such as internal transcribed spacer sequencing can also be performed for the identification of fungus, especially in immunocompromised individuals [6]. Combination of culture and microscopy has a higher chance of detecting fungal organisms [6].The treatment options include oral terbinafine (250 mg/day), itraconazole (100-200 mg/day), griseofulvin (250-500 mg/day), voriconazole, posaconazole, potassium iodide, local X-radiation, and topical 2-dimethylamino-6-(beta-diethylaminoethyl)-benzothiazole, with the most preferred drug being terbinafine [6]. The duration of treatment varies from one to six months, and treatment must be continued until the lesions have healed completely [6]. Cryotherapy has been tried in a few refractory cases [12,17].

Conclusions

The diagnosis of Majocchi’s granuloma is challenging due to the varied differential diagnoses requiring confirmation with the gold standard histopathological examination. Clinicians should consider Majocchi’s granuloma as a diagnosis for patients with nodular or granulomatous skin lesions especially following topical steroid use, immunosuppression, or trauma. The rampant use of topical steroids requires stringent policies to curb the availability of over-the-counter steroids and a call to action for healthcare providers for health education on topical steroid misuse. Early diagnosis and prompt treatment of superficial fungal infections play a pivotal role in preventing deeper infections such as Majocchi's granuloma.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 57-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Itchy skin lesions over the right ankle for 20 days
- **History of Present Illness:** Lesions developed and were treated with over-the-counter topical steroids starting the next day, initially reducing erythema but aggravating after five days, accompanied by nodule formation and serosanguinous discharge.
- **Past Medical History:** None provided
- **Personal History:** None provided
- **Family History:** None provided
- **Physical Examination:** Multiple ill-defined erythematous nodules (2-3 cm in size) with serosanguinous discharge and swelling of the right ankle; single annular hyperpigmented scaly patch noted below the right knee; afebrile with no lymphadenopathy and normal systemic examination.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Potassium hydroxide (KOH) examination: Positive for fungal hyphae.
  - Histopathological examination: Mixed inflammatory infiltrate with features suggestive of Majocchi's granuloma (lymphocytes, macrophages, epithelioid cells, neutrophils, plasma cells, and Langhans giant cells).
  - Fungal culture: Growth of *Trichophyton rubrum.*
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Considerations:** Conditions causing erythematous nodules affecting the extremities, such as erythema nodosum, mycetoma, furunculosis, and Majocchi's granuloma, were considered.
- **Rationale for Majocchi’s Granuloma as a Diagnosis:**
  - The history of topical steroid use, which exacerbates or induces deep fungal infections.
  - The KOH test confirmed fungal hyphae, indicating a fungal etiology.
  - Histopathology showed granulomatous perifollicular inflammation, a hallmark of Majocchi's granuloma.
  - Fungal culture identified *Trichophyton rubrum* as the causative organism, further confirming the diagnosis.
- **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
  - Erythema nodosum: Typically presents as painful subcutaneous nodules without fungal involvement.
  - Mycetoma: Characteristically associated with draining sinuses and grain discharge, which was absent here.
  - Furunculosis: Bacterial etiology, ruled out by the presence of fungal hyphae and culture confirming dermatophyte infection.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **Majocchi’s granuloma** caused by *Trichophyton rubrum* following misuse of over-the-counter topical steroids. This conclusion was based on clinical presentation, history of steroid use exacerbating the condition, positive fungal findings on KOH examination, histopathological confirmation of granuloma, and fungal culture.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's chief complaint: itchy skin lesions on the right ankle for 20 days. The history says they developed the lesions and used OTC topical steroids the next day. Initially, the redness got better but then worsened after five days with nodules and discharge.

First, the initial response to steroids but subsequent aggravation makes me think of a possible infection that wasn't controlled. Steroids can suppress the local immune response, which might allow an infection to spread deeper. The presence of nodules and serosanguinous discharge suggests a deeper inflammatory process, maybe a granulomatous reaction.

Looking at the physical exam: erythematous nodules with discharge and swelling. Also, there's an annular hyperpigmented scaly patch below the knee. Annular lesions often make me think of fungal infections like tinea corporis, but the location and other features might indicate something more.

Ancillary tests show KOH positive for hyphae, so there's a fungal element. The histopathology mentions mixed inflammatory infiltrate with features of Majocchi's granuloma, including Langhans giant cells. The culture grew Trichophyton rubrum, which is a common dermatophyte. 

Majocchi's granuloma is a deep fungal folliculitis, usually caused by dermatophytes like T. rubrum. It typically occurs when the fungus invades deeper into the dermis, often after trauma or inappropriate steroid use, which suppresses the local immune response. The nodules and granulomatous inflammation on histopathology fit this. The annular patch below the knee could be a superficial dermatophytosis that spread deeper when steroids were applied.

Putting it all together: The patient had a superficial fungal infection (tinea) treated with steroids, which allowed the fungus to invade deeper, leading to Majocchi's granuloma. The positive KOH, culture, and histopathology all confirm this. So the diagnosis should be Majocchi's granuloma caused by Trichophyton rubrum.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with itchy skin lesions on the right ankle that worsened after initial improvement with topical steroids. This paradoxical aggravation suggests a possible secondary infection or a deeper inflammatory process triggered by immunosuppressive effects of steroids.  
<step 2> Physical examination reveals erythematous nodules with serosanguinous discharge and swelling, alongside an annular hyperpigmented scaly patch. Annular lesions often indicate fungal infections (e.g., tinea corporis), while nodules and discharge suggest deeper tissue involvement, such as granulomatous inflammation.  
<step 3> The KOH examination confirms fungal hyphae, supporting a dermatophyte infection. Histopathology showing mixed inflammatory infiltrate with epithelioid cells, Langhans giant cells, and neutrophils aligns with Majocchi's granuloma, a deep fungal folliculitis.  
<step 4> Fungal culture identifying *Trichophyton rubrum*, a common dermatophyte, solidifies the etiology. The combination of superficial fungal infection (annular patch) and steroid-induced deep invasion (nodules with granulomatous features) confirms the diagnosis.  

### Answer:
Majocchi's granuloma due to *Trichophyton rubrum* infection.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with itchy skin lesions on the right ankle that worsened after initial improvement with topical steroids. This paradoxical aggravation suggests a possible secondary infection or a deeper inflammatory process triggered by immunosuppressive effects of steroids.

Physical examination reveals erythematous nodules with serosanguinous discharge and swelling, alongside an annular hyperpigmented scaly patch. Annular lesions often indicate fungal infections (e.g., tinea corporis), while nodules and discharge suggest deeper tissue involvement, such as granulomatous inflammation.

The KOH examination confirms fungal hyphae, supporting a dermatophyte infection. Histopathology showing mixed inflammatory infiltrate with epithelioid cells, Langhans giant cells, and neutrophils aligns with Majocchi's granuloma, a deep fungal folliculitis.

Fungal culture identifying *Trichophyton rubrum*, a common dermatophyte, solidifies the etiology. The combination of superficial fungal infection (annular patch) and steroid-induced deep invasion (nodules with granulomatous features) confirms the diagnosis.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial conditions causing erythematous nodules affecting the extremities were considered, including erythema nodosum, mycetoma, furunculosis, and Majocchi's granuloma.
The history of topical steroid use, which exacerbates or induces deep fungal infections, supports Majocchi’s granuloma.
KOH test confirmed fungal hyphae, indicating a fungal etiology consistent with Majocchi’s granuloma.
Histopathology showed granulomatous perifollicular inflammation, a hallmark of Majocchi's granuloma.
Fungal culture identified *Trichophyton rubrum* as the causative organism, further confirming the diagnosis.
Erythema nodosum typically presents as painful subcutaneous nodules without fungal involvement, ruling it out.
Mycetoma is characterized by draining sinuses and grain discharge, which were absent, ruling it out.
Furunculosis has a bacterial etiology, ruled out by the presence of fungal hyphae and culture confirming dermatophyte infection.
The final diagnosis is Majocchi’s granuloma caused by *Trichophyton rubrum* following misuse of over-the-counter topical steroids.
